title: Cervical Artery Dissection
oneLiner: Tear in the intimal wall of the carotid or vertebral artery → thrombus/emboli → stroke or TIA; classically in young patients with headache and ipsilateral Horner’s.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive MCA infarction** from carotid dissection → rapid hemiplegia, aphasia, GCS drop. *Single finding:* NIHSS >15 with dense hemiparesis and no improvement after 30 min.
- **Brainstem stroke / locked-in syndrome** from vertebral dissection → sudden quadriplegia, ophthalmoplegia, altered consciousness. *Single finding:* bilateral limb weakness + cranial nerve signs (e.g., dysarthria, diplopia) within minutes of presentation.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)** from intracranial extension of dissection (rare but catastrophic) → thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, photophobia. *Single finding:* CT head shows hyperdense blood in basal cisterns.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (the 5 you’ll see):**
  - Unilateral headache or neck pain – often severe, thunderclap or gradual, ipsilateral to dissection
  - Transient ischaemic attack (TIA) – especially amaurosis fugax (fleeting monocular vision loss) from carotid dissection
  - Horner’s syndrome – ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis on same side as pain
  - Focal neurological deficit – e.g., hemiparesis, dysphasia, ataxia, diplopia
  - Cranial nerve palsies (e.g., IX–XII) in carotid dissection; Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary) in vertebral dissection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Neurological: full NIHSS, cranial nerves (especially III, VI, IX–XII), limb power, coordination, gait
  - Vascular: carotid bruit (uncommon), auscultate over neck
  - Eye: check for ptosis, anisocoria in dim light (miosis on affected side)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Migraine with aura** – headache + transient neurological symptoms; *splitter:* Horner’s syndrome + neck pain is dissection, not migraine
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** – thunderclap headache + photophobia; *splitter:* CT head (clear in dissection unless intracranial extension)
- **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache + papilloedema + seizure; *splitter:* normal CT venogram / no neck vessel irregularity
- **Intracerebral haemorrhage** – acute deficit + hypertension; *splitter:* CT head shows blood, no vessel abnormality
- **Cluster headache** – severe unilateral head pain + autonomic features; *splitter:* Horner’s may be recurrent in cluster, but no ischaemic symptoms
- **Other stroke mimics** – hypoglycaemia, Todd’s paresis, functional disorder; *splitter:* blood glucose <3.5 or post-ictal
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - NIHSS scoring (baseline severity)
  - ECG – to exclude atrial fibrillation (embolic stroke)
  - Capillary glucose – rule out hypoglycaemic mimic
  - Check BP (permissive hypertension is common)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen, U+E, LFT, glucose
  - CRP, ESR – to screen for giant cell arteritis or vasculitis if fever/elderly
  - Lipid profile (baseline for secondary prevention)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (within 1 hour):** CT head → exclude haemorrhage, SAH. If negative, proceed to CT angiography (CTA) neck and circle of Willis – best initial test for dissection (intimal flap, double lumen, or tapered stenosis).
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain + MR angiography (MRA) neck – more sensitive for vessel wall haematoma (crescent sign) and ischaemic lesions; first choice if dissection suspected in young/low-intermediate risk.
  - **Gold standard:** Catheter angiography – reserved for equivocal CTA/MRA, or if endovascular intervention considered.
- **Special tests:**
  - Transoesophageal echocardiogram (if suspected patent foramen ovale or aortic dissection) – but not urgent unless alternative embolism suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single score; diagnosis based on imaging evidence of dissection: intimal flap, intramural haematoma (crescent sign), double lumen, or pseudoaneurysm on CTA/MRA.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CTA of neck vessels** (or MRA if CT contraindicated) – omission is a serious error; you cannot manage dissection without confirming the diagnosis and defining lesion extent.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC + stroke pathway:** open airway if GCS <8, treat hypoxia/hypotension
  - **Thrombolysis** (if ischaemic stroke with onset <4.5 hours and no contraindications) – do NOT withhold because of dissection; treat per local stroke protocol (discuss with stroke team)
  - **Antiplatelet therapy:** Aspirin 300 mg PO/PR stat (if not for thrombolysis) – standard first-line; some centres use dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for high-risk TIA/minor stroke (discuss with senior)
  - **Blood pressure management:** Do not lower BP aggressively initially (permissive hypertension up to 220/120 unless thrombolysed or haemorrhage). If thrombolysed, keep <180/105 for 24 hours.
  - **Imaging:** Expedite CTA neck if not done yet
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to **stroke unit** or high-dependency bed if large stroke/severe deficit
  - **Antiplatelet continuation:** Aspirin 75 mg OD (or clopidogrel 75 mg OD if aspirin intolerant)
  - **Anticoagulation:** Consider (warfarin or [check local policy for DOAC]) only in selected cases (e.g., recurrent ischaemic events despite antiplatelet, or large free-floating thrombus) – always discuss with stroke consultant
  - **Neuro-observations:** Hourly NIHSS or GCS + pupils for first 24h, then 4-hourly
  - **Nutrition:** NBM until swallow assessment; if dysphagia, NG feed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD as per local protocol – hold if anticoagulated or thrombolysed within 48h
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Antiplatelet for 3–6 months (minimum)** – then re-image (MRA or CTA) to confirm recanalisation; if vessel normal, stop antiplatelet; if residual stenosis or pseudoaneurysm, may continue lifelong aspirin or switch to anticoagulation
  - **Blood pressure, lipid, diabetes management** – standard secondary prevention for stroke
  - **Lifestyle advice** – avoid heavy lifting/straining, chiropractic manipulation
  - **Driving restrictions:** DVLA – must cease driving for 1 month after TIA, or 1 month after stroke if no residual deficit; discuss with patient
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 1 mL/kg/hr (maintain euvolaemia; avoid hypotonic fluids)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (start day after admission if no bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM** – NBM until swallow assessment by speech and language therapist; then free fluids → soft diet
  - **Observations:** neuro obs hourly (first 24h), then 4-hourly; NEWS2 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Aspirin* 300 mg PO/PR stat (loading), then 75 mg PO OD (maintenance)
  - *Clopidogrel* 300 mg PO stat (if aspirin intolerant), then 75 mg OD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Dual antiplatelet* (aspirin + clopidogrel) – only for 21 days (per CHANCE/POINT data) in high-risk TIA/minor stroke; discuss with stroke specialist
  - *Anticoagulation:* Warfarin (target INR 2–3) or [check local policy for DOAC] – use for high-risk dissection (free-floating thrombus, recurrent events, or pseudoaneurysm)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: active peptic ulcer, haemorrhage, severe hepatic impairment
  - Clopidogrel: severe hepatic impairment, active bleeding
  - Warfarin/DOAC: intracranial haemorrhage, uncontrolled hypertension, large stroke with haemorrhagic transformation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aspirin: dose adjustment for severe renal impairment (CrCl <10) – discuss with pharmacy
  - Clopidogrel: no dose adjustment needed
  - DOACs: adjust for renal function (see local policy)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Aspirin – giving ≥300 mg PO after thrombolysis** (risk of haemorrhage); after thrombolysis, start aspirin at 75 mg OD after 24h CT head shows no bleed.
  - **Warfarin/DOAC – starting in acute phase without confirming no haemorrhage** (convert a bland infarct into haemorrhagic transformation); always wait 24–48h after onset and check CT head before anticoagulation.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ischaemic stroke (most common) – mass effect, malignant MCA syndrome, brainstem infarction
  - Haemorrhagic transformation of infarct – risk increases with large infarct, anticoagulation, thrombolysis
  - Extension of dissection (proximal or distal) → worsening stenosis/occlusion
  - Pseudoaneurysm formation – may cause mass effect or thromboembolism
  - Subarachnoid haemorrhage (if dissection extends intracranially)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual neurological deficit (e.g., weakness, aphasia, ataxia)
  - Recurrent dissection (same or contralateral vessel) – 2–5% risk
  - Chronic pain (headache/neck pain) due to disrupted nerve fibres
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome if pseudoaneurysm thromboses
sections.Prognosis: - **Mortality:** 5–10% from acute stroke and complications. **Recovery:** ~60–70% achieve good functional outcome (mRS 0–2) with prompt treatment. **Recurrence:** low after 3 months (re-endothelialisation). Most dissections recanalise within 3–6 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check for Horner’s** in any young patient with one-sided headache + neck pain – it is the classic clue.
- **Document NIHSS on arrival** and recheck hourly to detect progression.
- **Ask about recent trauma** – even trivial (yoga, sports, vomiting) – and chiropractic manipulation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume it’s migraine** because the patient is young and has no focal signs – Horner’s + headache = dissect until proven otherwise.
- **Do not start anticoagulation without discussing with stroke consultant** unless clearly indicated – bleeding risk outweighs benefit in most cases.
- **Do not discharge with suspected dissection** even if imaging is negative → repeat MRA in 7 days if high suspicion (initial scan can be false negative).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New weakness, slurred speech, arm/leg numbness, double vision, difficulty swallowing, or severe new headache.
- **Follow-up:** Stroke clinic within 2 weeks; repeat vessel imaging (MRA/CTA) at 3–6 months to confirm recanalisation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if GCS drop of ≥2)
  - New focal neurological deficit or worsening of existing deficit (stroke in evolution)
  - Seizure (consider haemorrhagic transformation)
  - Deteriorating consciousness (GCS <9) → protect airway, call anaesthetics, urgent CT head
  - Signs of brainstem compression (gaze palsy, bilateral weakness, dysphagia, respiratory pattern changes) → immediate CT + stroke consultant
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate >2.5 (if sepsis concern)
  - Temperature >38°C (risk of worsened neurological outcome)
  - Systolic BP >220 mmHg despite IV labetalol or GTN infusion (consider ICU transfer)

rawMarkdown:
## Cervical Artery Dissection
**One-liner:** Tear in the intimal wall of the carotid or vertebral artery → thrombus/emboli → stroke or TIA; classically in young patients with headache and ipsilateral Horner’s.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive MCA infarction** from carotid dissection → rapid hemiplegia, aphasia, GCS drop. *Single finding:* NIHSS >15 with dense hemiparesis and no improvement after 30 min.
- **Brainstem stroke / locked-in syndrome** from vertebral dissection → sudden quadriplegia, ophthalmoplegia, altered consciousness. *Single finding:* bilateral limb weakness + cranial nerve signs (e.g., dysarthria, diplopia) within minutes of presentation.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)** from intracranial extension of dissection (rare but catastrophic) → thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, photophobia. *Single finding:* CT head shows hyperdense blood in basal cisterns.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (the 5 you’ll see):**
  - Unilateral headache or neck pain – often severe, thunderclap or gradual, ipsilateral to dissection
  - Transient ischaemic attack (TIA) – especially amaurosis fugax (fleeting monocular vision loss) from carotid dissection
  - Horner’s syndrome – ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis on same side as pain
  - Focal neurological deficit – e.g., hemiparesis, dysphasia, ataxia, diplopia
  - Cranial nerve palsies (e.g., IX–XII) in carotid dissection; Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary) in vertebral dissection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Neurological: full NIHSS, cranial nerves (especially III, VI, IX–XII), limb power, coordination, gait
  - Vascular: carotid bruit (uncommon), auscultate over neck
  - Eye: check for ptosis, anisocoria in dim light (miosis on affected side)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Migraine with aura** – headache + transient neurological symptoms; *splitter:* Horner’s syndrome + neck pain is dissection, not migraine
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** – thunderclap headache + photophobia; *splitter:* CT head (clear in dissection unless intracranial extension)
- **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache + papilloedema + seizure; *splitter:* normal CT venogram / no neck vessel irregularity
- **Intracerebral haemorrhage** – acute deficit + hypertension; *splitter:* CT head shows blood, no vessel abnormality
- **Cluster headache** – severe unilateral head pain + autonomic features; *splitter:* Horner’s may be recurrent in cluster, but no ischaemic symptoms
- **Other stroke mimics** – hypoglycaemia, Todd’s paresis, functional disorder; *splitter:* blood glucose <3.5 or post-ictal

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - NIHSS scoring (baseline severity)
  - ECG – to exclude atrial fibrillation (embolic stroke)
  - Capillary glucose – rule out hypoglycaemic mimic
  - Check BP (permissive hypertension is common)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen, U+E, LFT, glucose
  - CRP, ESR – to screen for giant cell arteritis or vasculitis if fever/elderly
  - Lipid profile (baseline for secondary prevention)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (within 1 hour):** CT head → exclude haemorrhage, SAH. If negative, proceed to CT angiography (CTA) neck and circle of Willis – best initial test for dissection (intimal flap, double lumen, or tapered stenosis).
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain + MR angiography (MRA) neck – more sensitive for vessel wall haematoma (crescent sign) and ischaemic lesions; first choice if dissection suspected in young/low-intermediate risk.
  - **Gold standard:** Catheter angiography – reserved for equivocal CTA/MRA, or if endovascular intervention considered.
- **Special tests:**
  - Transoesophageal echocardiogram (if suspected patent foramen ovale or aortic dissection) – but not urgent unless alternative embolism suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single score; diagnosis based on imaging evidence of dissection: intimal flap, intramural haematoma (crescent sign), double lumen, or pseudoaneurysm on CTA/MRA.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CTA of neck vessels** (or MRA if CT contraindicated) – omission is a serious error; you cannot manage dissection without confirming the diagnosis and defining lesion extent.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC + stroke pathway:** open airway if GCS <8, treat hypoxia/hypotension
  - **Thrombolysis** (if ischaemic stroke with onset <4.5 hours and no contraindications) – do NOT withhold because of dissection; treat per local stroke protocol (discuss with stroke team)
  - **Antiplatelet therapy:** Aspirin 300 mg PO/PR stat (if not for thrombolysis) – standard first-line; some centres use dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for high-risk TIA/minor stroke (discuss with senior)
  - **Blood pressure management:** Do not lower BP aggressively initially (permissive hypertension up to 220/120 unless thrombolysed or haemorrhage). If thrombolysed, keep <180/105 for 24 hours.
  - **Imaging:** Expedite CTA neck if not done yet
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to **stroke unit** or high-dependency bed if large stroke/severe deficit
  - **Antiplatelet continuation:** Aspirin 75 mg OD (or clopidogrel 75 mg OD if aspirin intolerant)
  - **Anticoagulation:** Consider (warfarin or [check local policy for DOAC]) only in selected cases (e.g., recurrent ischaemic events despite antiplatelet, or large free-floating thrombus) – always discuss with stroke consultant
  - **Neuro-observations:** Hourly NIHSS or GCS + pupils for first 24h, then 4-hourly
  - **Nutrition:** NBM until swallow assessment; if dysphagia, NG feed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD as per local protocol – hold if anticoagulated or thrombolysed within 48h
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Antiplatelet for 3–6 months (minimum)** – then re-image (MRA or CTA) to confirm recanalisation; if vessel normal, stop antiplatelet; if residual stenosis or pseudoaneurysm, may continue lifelong aspirin or switch to anticoagulation
  - **Blood pressure, lipid, diabetes management** – standard secondary prevention for stroke
  - **Lifestyle advice** – avoid heavy lifting/straining, chiropractic manipulation
  - **Driving restrictions:** DVLA – must cease driving for 1 month after TIA, or 1 month after stroke if no residual deficit; discuss with patient
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 1 mL/kg/hr (maintain euvolaemia; avoid hypotonic fluids)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (start day after admission if no bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM** – NBM until swallow assessment by speech and language therapist; then free fluids → soft diet
  - **Observations:** neuro obs hourly (first 24h), then 4-hourly; NEWS2 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Aspirin* 300 mg PO/PR stat (loading), then 75 mg PO OD (maintenance)
  - *Clopidogrel* 300 mg PO stat (if aspirin intolerant), then 75 mg OD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Dual antiplatelet* (aspirin + clopidogrel) – only for 21 days (per CHANCE/POINT data) in high-risk TIA/minor stroke; discuss with stroke specialist
  - *Anticoagulation:* Warfarin (target INR 2–3) or [check local policy for DOAC] – use for high-risk dissection (free-floating thrombus, recurrent events, or pseudoaneurysm)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: active peptic ulcer, haemorrhage, severe hepatic impairment
  - Clopidogrel: severe hepatic impairment, active bleeding
  - Warfarin/DOAC: intracranial haemorrhage, uncontrolled hypertension, large stroke with haemorrhagic transformation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aspirin: dose adjustment for severe renal impairment (CrCl <10) – discuss with pharmacy
  - Clopidogrel: no dose adjustment needed
  - DOACs: adjust for renal function (see local policy)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Aspirin – giving ≥300 mg PO after thrombolysis** (risk of haemorrhage); after thrombolysis, start aspirin at 75 mg OD after 24h CT head shows no bleed.
  - **Warfarin/DOAC – starting in acute phase without confirming no haemorrhage** (convert a bland infarct into haemorrhagic transformation); always wait 24–48h after onset and check CT head before anticoagulation.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ischaemic stroke (most common) – mass effect, malignant MCA syndrome, brainstem infarction
  - Haemorrhagic transformation of infarct – risk increases with large infarct, anticoagulation, thrombolysis
  - Extension of dissection (proximal or distal) → worsening stenosis/occlusion
  - Pseudoaneurysm formation – may cause mass effect or thromboembolism
  - Subarachnoid haemorrhage (if dissection extends intracranially)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual neurological deficit (e.g., weakness, aphasia, ataxia)
  - Recurrent dissection (same or contralateral vessel) – 2–5% risk
  - Chronic pain (headache/neck pain) due to disrupted nerve fibres
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome if pseudoaneurysm thromboses

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** 5–10% from acute stroke and complications. **Recovery:** ~60–70% achieve good functional outcome (mRS 0–2) with prompt treatment. **Recurrence:** low after 3 months (re-endothelialisation). Most dissections recanalise within 3–6 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check for Horner’s** in any young patient with one-sided headache + neck pain – it is the classic clue.
- **Document NIHSS on arrival** and recheck hourly to detect progression.
- **Ask about recent trauma** – even trivial (yoga, sports, vomiting) – and chiropractic manipulation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume it’s migraine** because the patient is young and has no focal signs – Horner’s + headache = dissect until proven otherwise.
- **Do not start anticoagulation without discussing with stroke consultant** unless clearly indicated – bleeding risk outweighs benefit in most cases.
- **Do not discharge with suspected dissection** even if imaging is negative → repeat MRA in 7 days if high suspicion (initial scan can be false negative).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New weakness, slurred speech, arm/leg numbness, double vision, difficulty swallowing, or severe new headache.
- **Follow-up:** Stroke clinic within 2 weeks; repeat vessel imaging (MRA/CTA) at 3–6 months to confirm recanalisation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if GCS drop of ≥2)
  - New focal neurological deficit or worsening of existing deficit (stroke in evolution)
  - Seizure (consider haemorrhagic transformation)
  - Deteriorating consciousness (GCS <9) → protect airway, call anaesthetics, urgent CT head
  - Signs of brainstem compression (gaze palsy, bilateral weakness, dysphagia, respiratory pattern changes) → immediate CT + stroke consultant
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate >2.5 (if sepsis concern)
  - Temperature >38°C (risk of worsened neurological outcome)
  - Systolic BP >220 mmHg despite IV labetalol or GTN infusion (consider ICU transfer)
